text: The patient is placed in a right lateral decubitus position.
The initial trocar site for placement of the thoracoscope is made in the seventh intercostal space, just anterior to the posterior axillary line.
Attention is given to achieving hemostasis so that there will be no blood dripping onto the camera lens.
Inspection of the left hemithorax is then performed.
A five centimeter anterior access incision is made in the fourth intercostal space.
The serratus muscle is divided along its fibers and the intercostal muscles divided on top of the rib.
A second 10 millimeter trocar is then inserted in the sixth intercostal space, posterior and 10 centimeters away from the initial CAM report.
A Teflon coated bovie is then used to divide the inferior pulmonary ligament.
A sponge stick is placed through the anterior axis incision to reflect the lung superiorly to allow adequate exposure to do this.
The inferior pulmonary vein is seen and the posterior mediastinal pleura between the aorta and esophagus and the lung parenchyma is then incised.

In this patient, where a left superior pulmonary segmentectomy will be performed for suspected metastatic papillary thyroid cancer, we need to identify the left superior segmental pulmonary vein first.
To facilitate this, a posterior station 10L node is dissected free from the surrounding structures and removed.
It is not uncommon to encounter some pesky bleeding from small bronchial arteries surrounding these lymph nodes.
These can either be controlled with electrocautery or more often we choose to use an endoclip as this tends to be more hemostatic.
The left superior segmental pulmonary vein is then dissected away from the underlying bronchus.
An endo-GIA stapler with 2.0 millimeter staples is then placed through the anterior access incision and used to staple and divide the vein.
Division of the vein allows more complete exposure of the inferior border of the pulmonary artery as it enters and defines the fissure.
Dissection along the pulmonary artery is then performed.
Sharp dissection can be performed with the aid of long Metzenbaum scissors through our access incision.
As one appreciates, the superior segmental artery is now identified more clearly.
There is an obvious early bifurcation of the superior segmental artery.
A stapler is inserted through the most posterior place trocar site.
This positioning allows relatively easy access to the superior segmental artery.
The stapler is then fired.
and divides the superior segmental artery.
Attention now turns to isolation of the superior segmental bronchus and all the corresponding lymph nodes adjacent to it.
One can appreciate how the remaining basilar segmental bronchi traverse the posterior aspect of the remaining inferior pulmonary vein.
Again, a tonsil clamp is used to complete the posterior aspect of the dissection.
An endo-GIA stapler with 3.5 millimeter staples is then used to divide the left superior segmental bronchus.
The lung is ventilated to ensure that only the superior segmental bronchus has been occluded.
Following division of the bronchus, the segmental hilum is dissected further to free up the posterior attachments of the bronchus which is being held with the operator's forceps.
The added time it takes to do this is minimal and it dramatically mobilizes the segment facilitating the subsequent parenchymal division.
The initial stapling of the lung parenchyma occurs along the fissure.
Grasping the segmental bronchovascular structures, the remaining parenchyma between the superior and basilar segments of the left lower lobe is divided.
The division of lung parenchyma is now completed with an additional application of an endo-GIA with 4.8 millimeter staples.
The specimen is placed in an endo-catch bag and removed from the hemithorax through the access incision.
Inspection of the bronchovascular staple lines reveals hemostasis and a complete removal of all nodal tissue.
A station 9 lymph node is now removed and sent to pathology for permanent sectioning.
No formal node dissection is performed in this patient with pathologic confirmed metastatic papillary thyroid cancer.
The bronchial margin and lung parenchymal margins all return with no evidence of malignancy on frozen section.
The bronchial stump and parenchyma are now tested for evidence of an air leak.
A single 24 French Blake drain is placed posterior and apically for drainage.
As one can see, the incision sites are quite small and cosmetically appealing.
This patient is undergoing a right superior segmentectomy for a solitary pulmonary nodule suspicious for lung cancer.
patient also has significant chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
The patient has been positioned in a left lateral decubitus position and the trochars are in place as discussed in the last video.
The dissection begins with division of the inferior pulmonary ligament.
The bovie electrocautery has been placed with the five centimeter anterior access incision.
The posterior mediastinal pleura is then incised in preparation of isolating the superior segmental pulmonary vein.
A station eight lymph node is identified and removed.
Note that this patient has significant emphysema and exposure can be difficult.
The surgeon needs to be patient and flexible with camera repositioning in order to ensure adequate exposure and visualization.
The posterior aspect of the right inferior pulmonary vein is now clearly seen.
In addition, one can begin to appreciate where the superior segmental pulmonary vein is.
A half-link Harken clamp is then used to isolate the superior segmental vein.
An endo-GIA 2.5 millimeter stapler
is then used to staple and transect the vein.
Attention now turns to identifying the right superior segmental bronchus.
Again, this patient's chronic obstructive pulmonary disease can make exposure difficult but it is doable.
With dissection, the superior segmental bronchus is now able to be appreciated.
A tonsil clamp is used to bluntly dissect between the bronchus and the underlying pulmonary artery.
An endokittener facilitates exposure of the tip of the tonsil clamp.
An endogia 3.5 millimeter staple is then used to divide the right superior segmental bronchus.
Prior to bronchial division, I asked the anesthesiologist to ventilate ever so slightly in patients with this degree of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
Aggressive ventilation in these patients only results in loss of exposure and frustration for the surgical team.
Division of the superior segmental bronchus now allows us to more fully appreciate the surrounding lymph nodes as well as the pulmonary artery.
Identification of the artery should be done by carefully dissecting near the bronchus itself.
With careful, albeit somewhat tedious, dissection, the superior segmental artery is now identified.
A right angle clamp is used to further expose and isolate the vessel.
The camera and posterior port sites are switched in order to facilitate placement of the endo-GIA stapler through the port where the camera was initially placed.
The superior segmental artery is stapled and divided.
Again, attention is paid to dissect the segmental hilar structures in order to ensure that all nodal tissue is taken with the specimen.
Note this lymph node, which is now identified and will be removed with cautery.
This was a partially calcified node from a prior histoplasmosis exposure.
Final pathology showed no evidence of malignancy.
A Swedish DeBakey clamp is then used to help compress the parenchyma prior to placement of the stapler.
Because of this gentleman's underlying COPD, peristrips are placed on the stapler.
The initial firing of the stapler is parallel to the oblique fissure.
The second application of the stapler is more posterior and further divides the parenchyma.
Again, I find the use of the Swedish DeBakey clamp to be helpful in terms of compressing the lung tissue prior to correct placement of the stapler.
As shown here, the EndoGIA stapler with peristrips now completes the segmentectomy.
All of the superior segmental bronchovascular structures and segmental nodes are included with the specimen.
The specimen is then placed into an endo-catch bag and removed.
Pathologic examination reveals an adenocarcinoma with all parenchymal and bronchial margins free of disease.
A station 7 nodal dissection is then performed as is done with any thoracoscopic lobectomy or segmentectomy.
Endoclips are used to facilitate hemostasis prior to removal of the nodal specimens.
The parenchymal staple lines and bronchial stump are tested for pneumostasis.
As one can see, there's absolutely no evidence of an air leak from the lung parenchyma or from the bronchial stump.
Again, the patient has one drainage tube in place with two port sites and a small anterior access incision.
